source.basis: metadata.glossary cross-referenced with metadata.questions[].glossaryRefs; explanations from i18n messages[<term>.context]

terms.context: The 5% threshold is a statutory ballot barrier in German electoral law: a party must receive at least 5 percent of second votes in the Bundestag (Federal Parliament) election to enter the Bundestag. This regulation is meant to prevent the parliament from fragmenting into many small factions. It thus serves political stability and facilitates the formation of governments capable of action.

matches.text: 5%-Hürde.
matches.translation: 5% hurdle.

terms.context: An Abendgymnasium is a state-recognized school of the second educational pathway where adults can earn their Abitur (university entrance certificate) alongside work or family. It is aimed at people who did not complete their schooling at the usual age. Classes take place in the evening and thus put into practice Germany's anchored right to education and lifelong learning.

matches.text: einem Abendgymnasium.
matches.translation: an evening high school.

matches.text: einem Abendgymnasium.
matches.translation: an evening high school.

terms.context: A delegate (Abgeordneter) is a member of parliament legitimized by the people through universal, free, equal, and secret elections who represents the entire people. According to Article 38 of the Grundgesetz (German Basic Law), Bundestag delegates are not bound by any orders or instructions from their party and are accountable only to their conscience. This free mandate secures the independence of the people's representation against party interests and is a core feature of the German parliamentary system.

matches.text: Im Parlament steht der Begriff "Opposition" für …
matches.translation: In Parliament the term “opposition” stands for…

matches.text: Wer wählt in Deutschland die Abgeordneten zum Bundestag?
matches.translation: Who elects the members of the Bundestag in Germany?

matches.text: eine Abgeordnete/ein Abgeordneter der stärksten Fraktion
matches.translation: one MP from the strongest parliamentary group

matches.text: Wie nennt man in Deutschland die Vereinigung von Abgeordneten einer Partei im Parlament?
matches.translation: What do you call the association of members of a party in parliament in Germany?

matches.text: Wenn Abgeordnete im Deutschen Bundestag ihre Fraktion wechseln, …
matches.translation: When members of the German Bundestag change their parliamentary group...

matches.text: Parlamentsmitglieder, die von den Bürgerinnen und Bürgern gewählt werden, nennt man …
matches.translation: Members of Parliament who are elected by citizens are called...

terms.context: Members of the European Parliament are directly elected by citizens of EU member states and represent their interests at the European level. They participate in European Union legislation and oversee the work of the European Commission. The European Parliament is thus the only directly elected body of the EU.

matches.text: Wer wird bei der Europawahl gewählt?
matches.translation: Who will be elected in the European elections?

terms.context: The Abitur is the highest general education school diploma in Germany and entitles the holder to study at universities and colleges. It is awarded after successful completion of the upper secondary level. In addition to the regular school route, the Abitur can also be earned through the second educational pathway—for example at an evening gymnasium—so that this qualification remains accessible to adults.

matches.text: das Abitur
matches.translation: the high school diploma

matches.text: Eine erwachsene Frau möchte in Deutschland das Abitur nachholen. Das kann sie an …
matches.translation: An adult woman wants to get her high school diploma in Germany. She can do that...

matches.text: Ein Mann möchte mit 30 Jahren in Deutschland sein Abitur nachholen. Wo kann er das tun? An …
matches.translation: A man wants to get his high school diploma in Germany at the age of 30. Where can he do that? On...

terms.context: Adolf Hitler was the chairman of the National Socialist Party (NSDAP) and became Chancellor of Germany in January 1933. He established a totalitarian dictatorship in which all state power was concentrated in his person—the so-called Führerprinzip (leadership principle)—and thereby ended the democratic order of the Weimar Republic. Under his rule, basic rights were abolished, political opponents were persecuted, and the Holocaust was systematically carried out.

matches.text: Wann waren die Nationalsozialisten mit Adolf Hitler in Deutschland an der Macht?
matches.translation: When did the National Socialists with Adolf Hitler come to power in Germany?

terms.context: Advent is the four weeks before December 25 and begins on the fourth Sunday before Christmas. It has Christian roots but has become a permanent part of social and cultural life in Germany. Typical customs include Advent wreaths, Advent calendars, and Christmas markets, which are practiced by people of different religious beliefs.

matches.text: die Adventszeit.
matches.translation: the Advent season.

terms.context: The AfD (Alternative for Germany) is a right-populist party that has been represented in the Bundestag (German Federal Parliament) since 2013 and forms an independent parliamentary faction there. It advocates political positions that are rejected by most other parties in the Bundestag, which shapes its role in parliamentary operations.

matches.text: CDU/CSU und AfD.
matches.translation: CDU/CSU and AfD.

terms.context: Active voting rights (aktives Wahlrecht) is the right to cast a vote in an election. In Germany, all German citizens aged 18 and over have this right. It differs from passive voting rights, which refers to the right to stand as a candidate in an election.

matches.text: Was bedeutet "aktives Wahlrecht" in Deutschland?
matches.translation: What does “active voting rights” mean in Germany?

matches.text: aktives Wahlrecht.
matches.translation: active right to vote.

terms.context: Following the unconditional surrender of Nazi Germany in May 1945, the four victorious powers of World War II—the USA, Soviet Union, Great Britain, and France—jointly assumed supreme governmental authority in Germany. They divided the country into four occupation zones, each administered by one of these powers. The Allied occupying powers thus formed, after the collapse of the Nazi state, the only legitimate state authority on German soil.

matches.text: Welche Länder wurden nach dem Zweiten Weltkrieg in Deutschland als "Alliierte Besatzungsmächte" bezeichnet?
matches.translation: Which countries were referred to as "Allied Occupying Powers" in Germany after World War II?

matches.text: Welches Land war keine "Alliierte Besatzungsmacht" in Deutschland?
matches.translation: Which country was not an "Allied occupying power" in Germany?

terms.context: A traffic-light coalition (Ampelkoalition) is a governing alliance of the SPD, FDP, and Alliance 90/The Greens, whose party colors red, yellow, and green together form the colors of a traffic light. In Germany, it is customary to name coalitions after the colors of the participating parties to make the alliance recognizable at a glance. Such color designations are not official names but established political shorthand.

matches.text: Was wird in Deutschland als "Ampelkoalition" bezeichnet? Die Zusammenarbeit …
matches.translation: What is called the “traffic light coalition” in Germany? The collaboration…

terms.context: In a market economy, supply and demand determine which goods are available in what quantities and at what prices. Businesses and consumers make their economic decisions largely freely, without central state planning. The state sets legal and social framework conditions to ensure fair competition and social balance.

matches.text: richtet sich nach Angebot und Nachfrage, aber der Staat sorgt für einen sozialen Ausgleich.
matches.translation: depends on supply and demand, but the state ensures social balance.

terms.context: A charge is the formal act by which the prosecutor brings a person before court for an alleged criminal offense. It initiates the actual criminal proceedings in which a court decides on guilt or innocence. Until a final judgment, the presumption of innocence applies—no one may be treated as guilty solely on the basis of a charge.

matches.text: Wann kommt es in Deutschland zu einem Prozess vor Gericht? Wenn jemand …
matches.translation: When does a trial take place in court in Germany? If someone...

terms.context: A claim is a legally protected right of a person against the state, a government agency, or another body that can, if necessary, be enforced in court. It differs from a mere support opportunity or discretionary benefit in that the obligated party is required to fulfill it. Claims may arise from the Grundgesetz (German Constitution), from federal laws such as the Social Code, or from other legal regulations.

matches.text: In Deutschland haben Kinder ab dem Alter von drei Jahren bis zur Ersteinschulung einen Anspruch auf …
matches.translation: In Germany, children from the age of three until they start school for the first time are entitled to...

terms.context: Antisemitism refers to hostility, prejudice, and discrimination against Jewish women and men. It was a core ideological element of Nazism and led to the systematic mass murder of millions of Jewish people in Europe. In Germany, antisemitism is socially condemned; certain forms of expression, such as denying the Holocaust, are prohibited by law.

matches.text: Was ist ein Beispiel für antisemitisches Verhalten?
matches.translation: What is an example of anti-Semitic behavior?

terms.context: A lawyer is a licensed legal professional who provides legal advice to citizens and represents them in court. In certain proceedings, such as a divorce before the family court, German procedural law requires mandatory legal representation, meaning at least one party must be represented by a lawyer. Lawyers are bound by confidentiality and professional ethics and form an important pillar of rule-of-law access to justice.

matches.text: die Unterstützung einer Anwältin/eines Anwalts
matches.translation: the support of a lawyer

terms.context: An employer is a person or company that employs others for compensation and has the authority to direct them. In the German social insurance system, employers are legally required to pay a set portion of their employees' contributions and remit them to the insurance carriers. German labor law also protects employees from arbitrary dismissals and binds employers to clear legal regulations.

matches.text: Arbeitgeberinnen/Arbeitgeber und Arbeitnehmerinnen/Arbeitnehmer
matches.translation: Employers and employees

matches.text: Was kann ich in Deutschland machen, wenn mir meine Arbeitgeberin/mein Arbeitgeber zu Unrecht gekündigt hat?
matches.translation: What can I do in Germany if my employer has unfairly dismissed me?

terms.context: Employees are persons who work in a dependent employment relationship for an employer on the basis of an employment contract in exchange for compensation. As the structurally weaker contracting party, they enjoy special protection under German labor law, such as through dismissal protection, minimum wage regulations, and working time rules. For collective representation of interests against employers, they have access to trade unions and the instrument of collective agreements; furthermore, they pay a legally set portion of social insurance contributions that is deducted directly from their wages.

matches.text: Arbeitgeberinnen/Arbeitgeber und Arbeitnehmerinnen/Arbeitnehmer
matches.translation: Employers and employees

matches.text: Arbeitnehmerinnen und Arbeitnehmer.
matches.translation: employees.

matches.text: Arbeitnehmerinnen und Arbeitnehmer
matches.translation: employees

matches.text: Welche Organisation in einer Firma hilft den Arbeitnehmerinnen und Arbeitnehmern bei Problemen mit der Arbeitgeberin/dem Arbeitgeber?
matches.translation: Which organization in a company helps employees with problems with the employer?

terms.context: The labor court is a specialized court for disputes between employees and employers, such as dismissals, wage claims, or collective labor law. It forms the first instance of the independent labor court system, above which stand the regional labor court and the federal labor court. This separate jurisdiction enables expert and expeditious resolution of labor law disputes.

matches.text: Sie gehen in Deutschland zum Arbeitsgericht bei …
matches.translation: You go to the labor court in Germany...

matches.text: das Arbeitsgericht
matches.translation: the labor court

terms.context: A labor law dispute is a legal disagreement between employees and employers over matters such as compensation, working conditions, or the legality of a dismissal. In Germany, there are separate labor courts that, as a specialized branch of the judiciary, ensure that both sides can enforce their claims equally before the court. This principle is part of the rule-of-law doctrine that all state powers are bound by law and every person has access to an independent court.

matches.text: Welches Gericht ist in Deutschland bei Konflikten in der Arbeitswelt zuständig?
matches.translation: Which court in Germany has jurisdiction over conflicts in the world of work?

terms.context: An employment relationship is the legal relationship between an employee and an employer that is created by an employment contract. It establishes mutual rights and obligations, including wage payment, working hours, and dismissal procedures. German labor law sets legal minimum standards that protect employees from unsuitable working conditions.

matches.text: Sie möchten bei einer Firma in Deutschland ihr Arbeitsverhältnis beenden. Was müssen Sie beachten?
matches.translation: You would like to end your employment relationship with a company in Germany. What do you have to consider?

terms.context: The right to asylum is a fundamental right under Article 16a of the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) and grants protection in Germany to foreigners who are politically persecuted. It is the only fundamental right in the Grundgesetz that applies exclusively to non-Germans from the outset because it concerns protection from persecution by a foreign state. Its inclusion in the Grundgesetz is closely connected to experiences from the Nazi period, when many people depended on finding refuge abroad.

matches.text: Welches Grundrecht gilt in Deutschland nur für Ausländerinnen/Ausländer? Das Grundrecht auf …
matches.translation: Which fundamental right only applies to foreigners in Germany? The fundamental right to…

terms.context: An assassination attempt is a targeted attack on a person, usually for political reasons. In German history, the assassination attempt of July 20, 1944 is particularly significant: resistance fighters tried to kill Hitler with a bomb to overthrow the Nazi dictatorship. The attempt failed; those involved were executed.

matches.text: das Attentat auf Hitler am 20. Juli 1944.
matches.translation: the assassination attempt on Hitler on July 20, 1944.

terms.context: Public calls for the destruction of the state of Israel are punishable in Germany and may be prosecuted as incitement to hatred under Section 130 of the German Criminal Code. The security of Israel is considered part of German reason of state, as Germany derives a special political and moral obligation from its responsibility for the Holocaust. This position reflects the lessons the German state has drawn from Nazi genocide policy.

matches.text: der öffentliche Aufruf zur Vernichtung Israels
matches.translation: the public call for the destruction of Israel

terms.context: An uprising is a collective rising of the population against political or economic conditions, often linked with demands for freedom and justice. The popular uprising of 17 June 1953 in the DDR (German Democratic Republic) is the most famous example in post-war German history: workers rose up against state repression and demanded political participation. Such uprisings often end with violent suppression by the state when democratic paths to change are not open.

matches.text: Im Jahr 1953 gab es in der DDR einen Aufstand, an den lange Zeit in der Bundesrepublik Deutschland ein Feiertag erinnerte. Wann war das?
matches.translation: In 1953 there was an uprising in the GDR that was commemorated by a public holiday in the Federal Republic of Germany for a long time. When was that?

terms.context: In Germany, vocational education (Ausbildung) refers above all to dual vocational training, in which young people acquire a recognized vocational qualification simultaneously at a workplace and at a vocational school. Such a qualification is considered an important foundation for access to skilled and well-paid positions. Since technological and economic change continuously transform the demands of the working world, lifelong learning alongside the initial qualification is becoming increasingly important.

matches.text: In Deutschland hat man die besten Chancen auf einen gut bezahlten Arbeitsplatz, wenn man …
matches.translation: In Germany you have the best chances of getting a well-paid job if you...

matches.text: Erwachsene müssen auch nach der Ausbildung immer weiter lernen.
matches.translation: Adults must continue to learn even after training.

terms.context: In legal terms, foreigners are all persons who do not hold German citizenship. The Grundgesetz (German Basic Law) distinguishes between rights belonging to everyone regardless of citizenship and rights reserved exclusively for German citizens. Asylum law holds a special position, as the Grundgesetz explicitly grants the right to asylum only to foreigners.

matches.text: Welches Grundrecht gilt in Deutschland nur für Ausländerinnen/Ausländer? Das Grundrecht auf …
matches.translation: Which fundamental right only applies to foreigners in Germany? The fundamental right to…

terms.context: Foreign policy comprises a state's relations with other countries and international organizations. In Germany, it is exclusively the responsibility of the Federation (Bund) — the 16 Länder (federal states) may not conduct independent foreign policy. This clear division of competence is anchored in the Grundgesetz and is an expression of German federalism (Föderalismus).

matches.text: Welche Senatorin/welchen Senator hat Berlin nicht?
matches.translation: Which senator does Berlin not have?

terms.context: Disability is explicitly named in Article 3, Section 3 of the Grundgesetz as a characteristic on account of which no one may be disadvantaged. This addition was incorporated into the Grundgesetz in 1994 and strengthens legal protection for people with disabilities against state and social exclusion. It is part of the general principle of equality that prohibits discrimination based on personal characteristics.

matches.text: Niemand darf z.B. wegen einer Behinderung benachteiligt werden.
matches.translation: No one should be disadvantaged because of a disability, for example.

terms.context: An authority (Behörde) is a state or municipal institution that carries out public tasks on behalf of the Federation, a Land (state), or a municipality and makes administrative decisions — such as tax offices, immigration authorities, or administrative offices. Under German administrative law, each authority may act only within its legally defined scope of responsibility and must comply with law and statute. Citizens have the right to contest administrative decisions using legally prescribed means, such as filing an objection (Widerspruch).

matches.text: bei einer Firma oder Behörde beschäftigt.
matches.translation: employed by a company or authority.

matches.text: eine Gaststättenerlaubnis von der zuständigen Behörde
matches.translation: a restaurant permit from the responsible authority

matches.text: Was sollten Sie tun, wenn Sie von Ihrer Ansprechpartnerin/Ihrem Ansprechpartner in einer deutschen Behörde schlecht behandelt werden?
matches.translation: What should you do if you are treated badly by your contact person in a German authority?

matches.text: Ich kann mich bei der Behördenleiterin/beim Behördenleiter beschweren.
matches.translation: I can complain to the head of the authority.

matches.text: Was tun Sie, wenn Sie eine falsche Rechnung von einer deutschen Behörde bekommen?
matches.translation: What do you do if you receive an incorrect invoice from a German authority?

matches.text: Ich lege Widerspruch bei der Behörde ein.
matches.translation: I lodge an objection with the authorities.

terms.context: The authority leadership (Behördenleitung) bears overall responsibility for the proper operation of a state authority and the conduct of its staff. In a Rechtsstaat (rule-of-law state), citizens have the right to lodge a complaint with the authority leadership if they feel they have been treated unfairly or poorly by a case officer. It is thus an important contact point for oversight of administrative action.

matches.text: Was sollten Sie tun, wenn Sie von Ihrer Ansprechpartnerin/Ihrem Ansprechpartner in einer deutschen Behörde schlecht behandelt werden?
matches.translation: What should you do if you are treated badly by your contact person in a German authority?

terms.context: After the Second World War, Berlin was divided into four occupation zones and became the central stage of the Cold War. While East Berlin served as the capital of the DDR (German Democratic Republic), West Berlin formed an enclave within the DDR and belonged to the Federal Republic of Germany. With the opening of the Wall in 1989 and reunification (Wiedervereinigung) in 1990, Berlin became the capital of a united Germany again.

matches.text: In welchem Jahr wurde die Mauer in Berlin gebaut?
matches.translation: In what year was the wall in Berlin built?

matches.text: Wann wurde die Mauer in Berlin für alle geöffnet?
matches.translation: When was the wall in Berlin opened to everyone?

matches.text: Von 1961 bis 1989 war Berlin …
matches.translation: From 1961 to 1989 Berlin was...

terms.context: The Berlin Wall (Berliner Mauer) was erected by the DDR on 13 August 1961 to stop mass flight of the population to the west, and divided Berlin into an eastern and a western part for 28 years. It stood as a symbol of German division and the Cold War. On 9 November 1989 it fell as a result of the peaceful revolution in the DDR and paved the way for reunification (Wiedervereinigung).

matches.text: Wer baute die Mauer in Berlin?
matches.translation: Who built the wall in Berlin?

matches.text: In welchem Jahr wurde die Mauer in Berlin gebaut?
matches.translation: In what year was the wall in Berlin built?

matches.text: Wann baute die DDR die Mauer in Berlin?
matches.translation: When did the GDR build the wall in Berlin?

matches.text: Wann wurde die Mauer in Berlin für alle geöffnet?
matches.translation: When was the wall in Berlin opened to everyone?

matches.text: Von 1961 bis 1989 war Berlin …
matches.translation: From 1961 to 1989 Berlin was...

terms.context: The Career Information Centre (Berufsinformationszentrum, or BIZ) is an institution of the Bundesagentur für Arbeit (Federal Employment Agency) that provides free information on occupations, vocational training pathways, and the labour market. It is aimed at young people and adults who wish to obtain information about training and employment opportunities. The BIZ is part of the state infrastructure for promoting employment and vocational guidance.

matches.text: Das Berufsinformationszentrum BIZ bei der Bundesagentur für Arbeit in Deutschland hilft bei der …
matches.translation: The BIZ career information center at the Federal Employment Agency in Germany helps with...

terms.context: An occupying power (Besatzungsmacht) is a state that militarily controls the territory of a defeated country after a war and exercises sovereign rights there. After the end of the Second World War, the four allied victorious powers divided Germany into four occupation zones and jointly assumed supreme governmental authority. From this division eventually emerged two German states: the Federal Republic of Germany (Bundesrepublik Deutschland) in the west and the German Democratic Republic (Deutsche Demokratische Republik) in the east.

matches.text: Welches Land war keine "Alliierte Besatzungsmacht" in Deutschland?
matches.translation: Which country was not an "Allied occupying power" in Germany?

terms.context: After Germany's unconditional surrender in May 1945, the four victorious powers — the USA, Great Britain, France, and the Soviet Union — divided the country into four occupation zones, in each of which that power exercised supreme governmental authority. Since each zone was administered according to the political principles of the respective occupying power, the zones developed very differently. From this division eventually emerged two German states: the Federal Republic of Germany (Bundesrepublik Deutschland) in the west and the German Democratic Republic (Deutsche Demokratische Republik) in the east.

matches.text: In welcher Besatzungszone wurde die DDR gegründet? In der …
matches.translation: In which occupation zone was the GDR founded? In the…

matches.text: sowjetischen Besatzungszone
matches.translation: Soviet occupation zone

matches.text: Wie viele Besatzungszonen gab es in Deutschland nach dem Zweiten Weltkrieg?
matches.translation: How many occupation zones were there in Germany after the Second World War?

matches.text: Wie waren die Besatzungszonen Deutschlands nach 1945 verteilt?
matches.translation: How were Germany's occupation zones distributed after 1945?

terms.context: The Betriebsrat (works council) is an employee-elected representation of interests within a company. It participates in social, personnel, and economic decisions of the employer on the basis of the Betriebsverfassungsgesetz (Works Constitution Act). This right of codetermination is meant to protect the rights of employees in the workplace and ensure a balanced relationship between employer and workforce.

matches.text: der Betriebsrat
matches.translation: the works council

terms.context: A Bezirk (district) is an administrative unit within a city-state like Berlin, which is simultaneously a Bundesland (federal state) and is divided into twelve such districts. Each district carries out independent administrative tasks and is led by an elected Bezirksverordnetenversammlung (district representatives' assembly) and a Bezirksamt (district office). The districts do not have their own parliament.

matches.text: Welches ist ein Bezirk von Berlin?
matches.translation: Which is a district of Berlin?

terms.context: The Bezirksverordnetenversammlung (district representatives' assembly, BVV) is the elected parliament at the level of a Berlin Bezirk (district). Berlin is divided into twelve districts, and each BVV supervises the associated district administration and participates in district decisions. Within Berlin's urban structure, it thus performs a similar function to a municipal parliament in other German cities.

matches.text: Ab welchem Alter darf man in Berlin bei Kommunalwahlen (Wahl der Bezirksverordnetenversammlung) wählen?
matches.translation: At what age are you allowed to vote in local elections (district council elections) in Berlin?

terms.context: Brandenburg is one of the five Bundesländer (federal states) newly established in 1990 after reunification on the territory of the former DDR (East Germany). It lies in northeastern Germany and geographically surrounds the capital Berlin, but constitutes an independent Bundesland. As part of the federal order of the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany), Brandenburg has its own state parliament and its own responsibilities.

matches.text: Brandenburg
matches.translation: Brandenburg

terms.context: A custom is an inherited action or practice that is regularly repeated in a community and thus creates cultural identity. Customs are often connected to religious or regional particularities and are passed down from generation to generation. They belong to the lived everyday life of society and reflect shared values and ways of living.

matches.text: Was ist in Deutschland ein Brauch zu Ostern?
matches.translation: What is a custom at Easter in Germany?

terms.context: The right to privacy of postal communications (Briefgeheimnis) is anchored in Article 10 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects the confidentiality of written communication from interference by the state and by private parties. It is one of the classic freedom rights that secure the privacy of the individual. Opening someone else's letter without authorization violates this basic right and is also a criminal offense under the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code).

matches.text: das Briefgeheimnis
matches.translation: the secrecy of letters

terms.context: In Briefwahl (postal voting), eligible voters cast their ballot in writing without visiting the polling station: they request official voting materials, fill out the ballot, and send it back to the responsible authority within the deadline. It is a legally equivalent alternative to voting in person and applies to Bundestag (Federal Parliament), state parliament, and municipal elections. Postal voting enables political participation regardless of presence on election day.

matches.text: Man kann durch Briefwahl seine Stimme abgeben.
matches.translation: You can cast your vote by postal vote.

terms.context: The Bund (Federation) refers to the national level of the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany), which exists alongside the 16 Bundesländer (federal states). Matters of general importance—such as foreign policy, defense, and federal legislation—fall within its responsibilities. The constitutional bodies of the Bund, including the Bundestag (Federal Parliament), Bundesrat (Federal Council), and Bundesregierung (Federal Government), act within the framework of the Grundgesetz for all citizens.

matches.text: Die Bundesrepublik Deutschland ist heute gegliedert in …
matches.translation: The Federal Republic of Germany is today divided into...

matches.text: Bund, Länder und Kommunen.
matches.translation: Federal, state and local governments.

terms.context: The Bundesadler (federal eagle) is the central state symbol of the Bundesrepublik and looks back on a centuries-old tradition of the eagle as a sign of authority and statehood in the German-speaking world. It appears both in the federal coat of arms and—in a more streamlined representation—in the plenary hall of the Bundestag, where it embodies the state sovereignty of Parliament.

matches.text: der Bundesadler
matches.translation: the federal eagle

terms.context: The Bundesagentur für Arbeit (Federal Employment Agency) is a federal public body based in Nuremberg. It supports people in transitioning between jobs by placing them in employment and apprenticeships, paying unemployment benefits, and providing vocational guidance. As part of the German welfare state, it contributes to social protection in cases of unemployment.

matches.text: Das Berufsinformationszentrum BIZ bei der Bundesagentur für Arbeit in Deutschland hilft bei der …
matches.translation: The BIZ career information center at the Federal Employment Agency in Germany helps with...

terms.context: The Bundesflagge (federal flag) is the official state symbol of the Bundesrepublik and displays three horizontal stripes in black, red, and gold. These colors derive from the German freedom movement of the 19th century and were established as national colors in 1949 with the Grundgesetz. They stand for the democratic values and the state unity of Germany.

matches.text: Welche Farben hat die deutsche Flagge?
matches.translation: What colors is the German flag?

terms.context: The Bundeskabinett (federal cabinet) consists of the federal chancellor (Bundeskanzler/Bundeskanzlerin) and all federal ministers and thus forms the Bundesregierung (Federal Government) as a whole. The ministers are formally appointed by the Bundespräsident (Federal President) on the proposal of the chancellor. As the central executive organ, the cabinet jointly makes the essential political decisions at the federal level.

matches.text: das Bundeskabinett zu bilden.
matches.translation: to form the federal cabinet.

matches.text: Wer leitet das deutsche Bundeskabinett?
matches.translation: Who heads the German federal cabinet?

terms.context: The Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor) is the head of government of the Federal Republic of Germany and leads the Bundesregierung (Federal Government). According to Article 63 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law), he is elected by the Bundestag (Federal Parliament) on the proposal of the Bundespräsident (Federal President). He holds policy direction authority under Article 65 of the Grundgesetz, meaning the right to set fundamental guidelines for government policy, and he bears responsibility for this to parliament.

matches.text: Der deutsche Bundespräsident Gustav Heinemann gibt Helmut Schmidt 1974 die Ernennungsurkunde zum deutschen Bundeskanzler. Was gehört zu den Aufgaben der deutschen Bundespräsidentin/des deutschen Bundespräsidenten? © Bundesregierung/Engelbert Reineke
matches.translation: The German Federal President Gustav Heinemann gives Helmut Schmidt the certificate of appointment as German Chancellor in 1974. What are the tasks of the German Federal President? © Federal Government/Engelbert Reineke

matches.text: Wie heißt die jetzige Bundeskanzlerin/der jetzige Bundeskanzler von Deutschland?
matches.translation: What is the name of the current Chancellor of Germany?

matches.text: Wer wählt die Bundeskanzlerin/den Bundeskanzler in Deutschland?
matches.translation: Who elects the Chancellor in Germany?

matches.text: die Bundeskanzlerin/der Bundeskanzler
matches.translation: the Federal Chancellor

matches.text: Wer wählt die deutsche Bundeskanzlerin/den deutschen Bundeskanzler?
matches.translation: Who will elect the German Chancellor?

matches.text: Wie hieß der erste Bundeskanzler der Bundesrepublik Deutschland?
matches.translation: What was the name of the first Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany?

matches.text: Der erste Bundeskanzler der Bundesrepublik Deutschland war …
matches.translation: The first Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany was...

terms.context: The Bundeskanzleramt (Federal Chancellery) is the official seat and workplace of the Bundeskanzler in Berlin. It coordinates the work of the Bundesministerien (Federal Ministries) and is the organizational center of the Bundesregierung (Federal Government). As part of the executive, it contributes to implementing the government's political decisions and ensuring coordination among the individual departments.

terms.context: A Bundesland (Federal State) is a constituent state of the Federal Republic of Germany with its own constitution, parliament, and government. Germany comprises 16 Bundesländer, which have independent responsibilities in areas such as education, police, and culture and participate in legislation at the federal level through the Bundesrat. After reunification in 1990, five new Bundesländer were established in the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic), which increased the total from ten to sixteen.

terms.context: Federal politics encompasses the political decisions and legislative procedures at the level of the German state as a whole. In Germany's federal system, the Bund and the Länder (states) are closely interwoven: the Bundesrat (Federal Council) gives the Länder a direct voice in federal legislation. Through this, the majority relationships at state level directly affect the political options available to the federal government.

terms.context: The Bundespräsident (Federal President) is the head of state of the Federal Republic of Germany and one of the five constitutional bodies. He represents the state internally and externally, acts as an integrating force in social and political conflicts, and provides impulses for public debate—without assuming government responsibilities. He is not elected directly by the people but by the Bundesversammlung, to deliberately keep the office separate from party-political disputes.

terms.context: The Bundesrat (Federal Council) is one of the five constitutional bodies of the Federal Republic of Germany and represents the interests of the 16 Bundesländer at the federal level. It consists of members of the Landesregierungen (State Governments), whose voting weight depends on the population of each state. For certain federal laws, the Bundesrat must approve or can lodge an objection—it is thus a central element of German federalism and secures the participation of the Länder in national legislation.

It directs the policy of the Bund, implements laws, and depends on a stable majority in the Bundestag for doing so. Its members are formally appointed and dismissed by the Bundespräsident.

terms.context: The Federal Republic of Germany is the official name of the democratic state established in 1949 in the territory of the western occupation zones, whose constitution is the Grundgesetz. The name combines the form of state (republic) with the federal structure: the Bund, Länder, and municipalities share state power. With reunification in 1990, the DDR (German Democratic Republic) joined this Federal Republic, which has comprised all 16 Bundesländer since then.

terms.context: The Bundestagspräsident (President of the Federal Parliament) directs the plenary sessions of the Bundestag and represents parliament to the outside world. The office is considered the second-highest state office of the Federal Republic of Germany, after the Bundespräsident. By parliamentary tradition, it is assumed by a member of the largest faction in the Bundestag.

terms.context: The Bundestagssitz (Seat of the Federal Parliament) is the location where the German parliament, the Bundestag, convenes and performs its duties as the supreme legislative body. Since 1999, the Bundestag has met in the Reichstag building in Berlin, to which it moved from Bonn in the course of German reunification. This move was also a symbolic step in the return of capital functions to Berlin.

terms.context: A Bundestag election is the election to the German Bundestag (Federal Parliament), the central legislative body of the Federal Republic of Germany, which takes place regularly every four years. The electoral principles – universal, direct, free, equal, and secret – are enshrined in Article 38 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) and realize the principle of popular sovereignty. The active right to vote belongs to all German citizens from age 18.

terms.context: The Order of Merit of the Federal Republic of Germany is the highest state decoration of the Federal Republic of Germany for services by civilians. It belongs to the Order of Merit of the Federal Republic of Germany, which was founded in 1951, and is awarded by the Federal President. Recognized are special achievements in political, economic, cultural, intellectual, or social fields.

terms.context: The Federal Constitutional Court is the highest court of the Federal Republic of Germany and ensures that laws, government measures, and actions by constitutional bodies are compatible with the Basic Law. Citizens can petition it with a constitutional complaint if they believe their fundamental rights have been violated. As the only body, it may also impose a ban on a party if that party actively opposes the free democratic basic order.

matches.text: Wann kann in Deutschland eine Partei verboten werden?

matches.text: Bundesverfassungsgericht

terms.context: The Federal Assembly is a constitutional body that convenes exclusively to elect the Federal President. It consists of all members of the Bundestag and an equal number of delegates sent by the parliaments of the 16 federal states. This procedure reflects Germany's federal structure by involving both the federal government and the states in the election of the head of state.

matches.translation: the Federal Assembly

terms.context: The Federal Election Ordinance (BWO) is a legal regulation that supplements the Federal Electoral Law and establishes the practical implementation rules for Bundestag elections. It regulates, among other things, the design and validity of ballot papers as well as organizational procedures surrounding the election. As a sub-legislative legal instrument, it specifies the legal requirements without being a formal law itself.

matches.translation: Which ballot paper would be valid in a federal election? Based on the Federal Electoral Regulations (BWO), Appendix 26

terms.context: The Bundeswehr is the armed forces of the Federal Republic of Germany and was founded in 1955. It operates under the primacy of politics: in peacetime it is subject to parliamentary control by the Bundestag, in case of defence it comes under the command of the Federal Chancellor. The principle of the 'citizen in uniform' ensures that soldiers retain their fundamental rights as responsible citizens.

matches.text: Was ist die Bundeswehr?
matches.translation: What is the Bundeswehr?

terms.context: Alliance 90/The Greens is a German party that emerged in 1993 from the merger of the West German Greens and the East German Alliance 90. It advocates especially for environmental and climate policy as well as for civil rights issues. The party is one of those represented in the German Bundestag and belongs to the democratic party spectrum of the Federal Republic.

matches.text: von SPD, FDP und Bündnis 90/Die Grünen in einer Regierung
matches.translation: of SPD, FDP and Alliance 90/The Greens in one government

terms.context: A citizens' initiative is a voluntary association of citizens who jointly represent a concrete political or social concern. It enables democratic participation outside of parties and elections, for instance through petitions, signature campaigns, or public actions. Citizens' initiatives are a recognized means of civil society participation at the municipal, state, and federal level.

matches.text: Ich beteilige mich an einer Bürgerinitiative für die Erhaltung der Buslinie oder gründe selber eine Initiative.
matches.translation: I take part in a citizens' initiative to maintain the bus route or start an initiative myself.

terms.context: A mayor is the elected head of a municipality and directs the local administration. He or she represents the municipality to the outside world and implements the decisions of the municipal council. The exact duties and powers are set out in the municipal ordinances of the respective federal states, which is why they can vary slightly from state to state.

terms.context: A citizens' assembly is a form of direct democracy at the municipal level in which residents consult together on local matters. It is one of the means of political participation below the parliamentary level but has no independent state decision-making power. Unlike the constitutional bodies of the federal government, it is not anchored in the Basic Law.

matches.translation: the citizens' assembly

terms.context: The CDU (Christian Democratic Union) is one of Germany's major people's parties and is located in the center-right conservative political spectrum. Since the founding of the Federal Republic in 1949, it has been a central actor in political will formation and has repeatedly participated in the federal government. In Bavaria, the CSU assumes a comparable role as an independent sister party; in all other federal states, the CDU is active.

terms.context: The CDU (Christian Democratic Union) and the CSU (Christian Social Union) are two separate parties that appear in the Bundestag as a joint parliamentary group – the Union faction. The CDU runs candidates in all federal states except Bavaria, while the CSU runs exclusively in Bavaria. Both parties rank among the major political forces in the German party system and stand for Christian democratic values.

terms.context: Christianity is one of the world's most widespread religions and has fundamentally shaped law, culture, and social values in Europe over centuries. In Germany, both major denominations – Catholicism and Protestantism – influenced education, the calendar, and the foundations of the legal system. The freedom of faith and religion in the Basic Law today protects the right of all people to belong to a religion or to none.

terms.context: The Christian Social Union (CSU) is a political party that runs exclusively in Bavaria and is therefore the only large German party limited to a single state. It is positioned in the center-right political spectrum and is considered the sister party of the CDU, with which it cooperates closely at the federal level. Both parties traditionally form a joint parliamentary faction in the Bundestag (German parliament).

terms.context: A demonstration is a public assembly in which people collectively express their opinion or political demands. terms.context: Discrimination refers to the unjustified disadvantage of a person based on personal characteristics such as origin, skin color, gender, or disability. The experiences of this violent dictatorship fundamentally shaped the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) and the democratic order of the Federal Republic of Germany.

terms.context: Marriage in Germany is a legally recognized bond between two people, established through a civil ceremony. terms.context: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) guarantees parents in Article 6 the primary right to care for and educate their children. terms.context: Parental allowance is a state social benefit that provides financial security to parents after the birth of a child so they can dedicate themselves to childcare. It is part of the German welfare state principle and must be actively requested from the responsible authority — it is not paid automatically.

It is typically located in the welfare or social services offices of the Länder (federal states), as the implementation of the Federal Parental Allowance Act is the responsibility of the states. Parents contact this office after the birth of a child to apply for the income replacement benefit.

terms.context: A dismissal is the unilateral termination of an employment relationship by the employer. terms.context: The Euro is the common currency of the European Union and replaced the Deutsche Mark as Germany's official means of payment on January 1, 2002. terms.context: The European Parliament is the only organ of the European Union that is directly elected by eligible citizens of the member states. Its official seat is in Strasbourg, chosen as a symbol of Franco-German reconciliation after the Second World War; additional workplaces are Brussels and Luxembourg.

terms.context: In European Parliament elections, citizens of EU member states directly elect the members of the European Parliament every five years. It is the only directly democratic election at the European level, in which the population of the entire Union determines its political representation. Germany's social insurance system is based on the solidarity principle: employers and employees share the contributions.

terms.context: Federalism is a constitutional principle of the Federal Republic of Germany, according to which state tasks are divided between the Bund (federal government) and the 16 Länder (federal states). Certain policy areas – such as education policy – fall under the independent jurisdiction of the Länder, without the Bund being able to directly pass laws there. terms.context: A Freiheitsstrafe (prison sentence) is a sanction imposed by a court in which the convicted person is deprived of personal liberty through imprisonment. terms.context: Legislation is the process through which binding legal norms are created. In the German system, responsibility for laws is divided between the federal government and the Länder (states), with the Bundesrat (Federal Council) bringing the interests of the states to bear at the federal level. terms.context: The separation of powers divides state power into three independent areas: the legislature (legislation), the executive (administration and government), and the judiciary (adjudication). This principle prevents the concentration of power in a single institution and is regarded as an essential feature of the rule of law in the Grundgesetz. It thus protects the freedom and constitutional rights of all people from arbitrary state action.

terms.context: A trade union is an organization that represents the economic and social interests of workers against employers and the state. terms.context: A ghetto was a sealed-off and heavily guarded city district where Nazi occupiers forcibly imprisoned Jewish people during World War II. The residents lived there under extreme crowding, hunger, and violence before being deported to extermination camps. The Warsaw Ghetto Uprising of 1943 stands as a symbol of armed resistance against this policy of annihilation.

terms.context: Article 4 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) guarantees everyone the right to freely choose, profess, and practice a religion or worldview. This fundamental right protects both religious convictions and non-religious conscience decisions, and applies to believers as well as people without religious affiliation. The state may not force anyone to hold a particular belief or disadvantage them because of their religious or ideological conviction.

terms.context: Equal treatment means that all people, regardless of origin, skin color, gender, or religion, enjoy the same legal protection and may not be disadvantaged. Article 3 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) anchors this principle as a fundamental right and prohibits discrimination by both the state and private actors. The Allgemeine Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (General Equal Treatment Act, AGG) supplements this protection in everyday life, for example in access to goods, services, and employment.

matches.translation: Equal treatment

terms.context: The principle of equal treatment is anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and obligates the state to treat all people equally regardless of characteristics such as origin, gender, religion, or disability. It protects against arbitrary disadvantage by state authorities and extends into social and labor-law areas as well. In the Allgemeine Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (AGG), this principle has taken on concrete legal form.

matches.text: Was bedeutet in Deutschland der Grundsatz der Gleichbehandlung?
matches.translation: What does the principle of equal treatment mean in Germany?

terms.context: Equal rights means that all people have the same rights and opportunities regardless of their gender. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) explicitly anchors this principle and thus obligates both state and society to prevent disadvantage based on gender. In working life, this protects women, for example, against dismissal or mistreatment because of pregnancy or other gender-specific circumstances.

matches.translation: A woman in Germany loses her job. What should not be the reason for this dismissal?

terms.context: Equal voting is a constitutional principle that ensures every vote cast has the same counting value. No voter may be favored or disadvantaged. This principle belongs to the electoral principles of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects democratic participation from unequal weighting of votes.

matches.translation: What principle are elections in Germany subject to? Elections in Germany are...

terms.context: Division refers to the organization of the state into various levels, each with its own responsibilities and powers. In Germany, this division is anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and follows the principle of federalism, which provides for the Bund (federal government), the Länder (states), and municipalities as independent political levels. This structure prevents concentration of power and secures democratic control at multiple levels.

matches.text: Die Bundesrepublik Deutschland ist heute gegliedert in …
matches.translation: The Federal Republic of Germany is today divided into...

terms.context: A state border defines the spatial jurisdiction of a state and is the basis of its sovereignty. Germany's present-day borders were definitively fixed by international treaties after reunification. This ended the postwar order, and Germany permanently recognized all existing borders with its neighboring states.

matches.translation: The Federal Republic of Germany has had today's borders since...

terms.context: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) is the constitution of the Federal Republic of Germany and came into force on May 23, 1949. The name was deliberately chosen to emphasize the provisional character of the document during German division; after reunification in 1990, it was retained. It establishes fundamental rights, state principles, and the structure of constitutional organs, and serves as the legal standard by which all state actions are measured.

terms.context: Fundamental rights are rights anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) that protect individuals from state interference and directly bind all state powers. Most fundamental rights apply to all people in Germany regardless of their citizenship; however, some so-called Deutschenrechte (rights reserved for Germans) apply only to German citizens. The core of fundamental rights is permanently secured by the eternity clause (Article 79, Section 3 of the Grundgesetz) and cannot be abolished even by a constitutional majority.

terms.context: A founding member is a state that co-founded an international organization from the beginning and participated in drafting its foundational treaties. The Federal Republic of Germany was among six states that signed the Treaties of Rome in 1957, thereby laying the foundation for today's European Union. This early membership was an expression of the political will to institutionally anchor lasting peace and cooperation in Europe after the Second World War.

matches.translation: The Federal Republic of Germany is a founding member...

terms.context: House rules are a binding set of regulations for communal living in multi-family buildings. They establish duties and behavioral standards for all residents, for example concerning quiet hours, use of common areas, and cleanliness in the building. Legally, house rules are usually part of the rental agreement or provided as a separate agreement and apply equally to all persons in the building.

terms.context: Adolf Hitler was the Führer (leader) of the National Socialist movement and ruled Germany from 1933 to 1945 as a dictator. Under his rule, democratic fundamental rights were abolished, millions of people were systematically persecuted and murdered, and Europe was plunged into catastrophe by the Second World War. The experiences of this dictatorship shape the German Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and the commitment to defensive democracy to this day.

matches.translation: the assassination attempt on Hitler on July 20, 1944.

terms.context: The Holocaust designates the systematic persecution and murder of approximately six million Jews and millions of other people by the Nazi regime. This crime is a central point of reference in German memory culture and in responsibility for democracy and human rights. In Germany, publicly denying or downplaying the Holocaust is punishable as Volksverhetzung (incitement to hatred) under § 130 of the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code), because it contradicts historical truth and violates the dignity of the victims.

terms.context: An interest group is an organization that unites the common concerns of a particular social group and represents them to politics, the economy, and the public. In the labor world, trade unions perform this function for employees, while employer associations represent the interests of businesses. Interest groups are a permanent part of civil society in a pluralistic democracy.

matches.translation: Trade unions are interest groups for...

terms.context: Jews are members of the Jewish people, who experienced discrimination and persecution in Europe over centuries. Under National Socialism, Jews were systematically disenfranchised and murdered in the Holocaust. Since 1948, when the State of Israel was founded as a Jewish state, its security has been considered part of German Staatsräson (state interest).

terms.context: The judiciary is the judicial power in the state and, alongside the legislature and executive, forms one of the three powers in the principle of separation of powers. In Germany, independent courts and judges exercise this power by applying laws to specific cases. In doing so, they also control the other state powers and thus protect the fundamental rights of citizens.

matches.translation: Judiciary

terms.context: A Jewish community is an organized community of Jewish citizens that sustains religious, cultural, and social life. Jewish communities can be documented in the territory of today's Germany since approximately the 4th century, making Jews one of the oldest population groups in the country. After their near-complete annihilation under National Socialism, Jewish communities in Germany were rebuilt after 1945 and grew again in the following decades through immigration.

terms.context: The Jugendamt (youth office) is a municipal authority that advises families with parenting difficulties, offers support for parenting, and protects the wellbeing of children and young people. Its tasks are regulated in the Child and Youth Services Act (SGB VIII); its use is voluntary and confidential as long as the welfare of the child is not endangered. If the child's welfare is endangered—for example through abuse or neglect—the Jugendamt can intervene protectively within the framework of the state's guardian role under the Grundgesetz (Constitution).

terms.context: The Cold War was a global power struggle between the Western democracies led by the USA and the Eastern Bloc led by the Soviet Union, which shaped international politics from 1945 to 1991. Although there was no direct war between the superpowers, ideology, armament, and political influence were used as weapons. Germany was divided by this conflict: the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany) belonged to the Western alliance, and the DDR (German Democratic Republic) to the socialist Eastern Bloc.

matches.text: Zu wem gehörte die DDR im "Kalten Krieg"?
matches.translation: Who did the GDR belong to in the "Cold War"?

terms.context: Capitulation is the formal military submission of a state to its opponent. With the unconditional capitulation on May 8, 1945, Germany handed over its armed forces without reservation to the Allied powers and thus lost any say in the post-war order. It formed the legal foundation for the division of Germany into occupation zones and for the subsequent political reorganization of the country.

matches.translation: through the unconditional surrender of Germany

terms.context: The Grundgesetz (Constitution) establishes in Article 6 the care and education of children as the natural right of parents and assigns them primary responsibility for their children's upbringing. The state assumes a supplementary guardian role and may only intervene if the child's welfare is endangered. This principle reflects the relationship between familial self-responsibility and the state's protective duty.

matches.text: Wer ist in Deutschland hauptsächlich verantwortlich für die Kindererziehung?
matches.translation: Who is primarily responsible for raising children in Germany?

terms.context: Kindergarten is a state-recognized institution for early childhood education and care for children from age three until school entry. It is operated by municipalities, independent, or denominational providers and is part of the public education system. The offering supports child development and helps families balance family and work.

terms.context: Church tax (Kirchensteuer) is a levy that tax-authorized religious communities are permitted to collect from their members. terms.context: On December 7, 1970, Chancellor Willy Brandt knelt at the memorial of the Warsaw Ghetto—a silent, unplanned gesture of sorrow and humility. It is considered one of the most significant moments in German memory culture in relation to the victims of the Nazi dictatorship.

terms.context: A coalition is an alliance of several parties that form a government together after an election and jointly carry out its policy. It arises because in German parliamentarism, no single party regularly achieves an absolute majority of seats. The parties involved set out their joint political objectives in a coalition agreement.

terms.context: In local elections, citizens elect the people's representatives at the local level, such as in municipalities, cities, or city districts. terms.context: Municipalities—cities, towns, and counties—form the lowest level of state organization in Germany. They are responsible for many tasks of daily life, including kindergartens, local infrastructure, and the registration system. The Grundgesetz (German Constitution) guarantees municipalities the right to self-governance, so they can regulate their local affairs independently.

terms.context: Health insurance is a central part of the German social insurance system and ensures access to medical care for all insured persons. In Germany, most working people are subject to compulsory statutory insurance, with contributions shared by employees and employers. This principle of solidarity ensures that health services can be used regardless of individual income.

matches.translation: the health insurance

terms.context: A dismissal is a unilateral declaration to end an employment relationship—it can be made by an employer or by an employee. In Germany, the Protection Against Wrongful Dismissal Act protects employees from arbitrary job loss by requiring that a dismissal must be objectively justified. If a dismissal violates this or other statutory provisions, it is considered unjustified and can be challenged before the labor court.

matches.translation: unjustified termination by your boss.

terms.context: A notice period is the legally or contractually prescribed period of time that must pass between the dismissal of an employment relationship and its actual end. It protects both employees and employers from an abrupt termination of the employment relationship and gives both sides time to adjust to the new situation. Under German labor law, the statutory minimum notice period for employees is four weeks; for employers, longer periods apply depending on the duration of employment.

matches.text: die Kündigungsfrist
matches.translation: the notice period

terms.context: A wrongful dismissal suit is a labor law remedy by which employees can have a dismissal reviewed before a labor court to determine whether it is lawful. If the dismissal is declared invalid, the employment relationship continues. The suit must typically be filed within three weeks of receipt of the dismissal notice.

matches.text: Kündigungsschutzklage erheben
matches.translation: Bring a dismissal protection claim

terms.context: Germany is divided into 16 Bundesländer (federal states), each of which has its own parliament and government. The states are mainly responsible for areas such as education, police, and culture. Through the Bundesrat (Federal Council), they jointly influence the legislation of the federal government.

matches.translation: The Federal Republic of Germany is today divided into...

matches.translation: Federal, state and local governments.

terms.context: A state flag is the official emblem of a German Bundesland (federal state) and an expression of its federal independence within the Federal Republic. It differs from the federal flag and typically bears the historical colors or symbols of the respective state. In Germany's federal system, all 16 Bundesländer have their own emblems, including a flag and coat of arms.

matches.text: Welche Farben hat die Landesflagge von Berlin?
matches.translation: What colors does the state flag of Berlin have?

terms.context: A state parliament is the elected people's representative body of one of Germany's sixteen Bundesländer (federal states). It passes laws applicable in the respective state and supervises the state government. Depending on the state, it carries its own name—in Berlin it is called the Abgeordnetenhaus, in Bavaria the Landtag, and in Hamburg the Bürgerschaft.

matches.translation: For how many years is Berlin's state parliament elected?

terms.context: Each of Germany's 16 Bundesländer (federal states) has its own state government, led by a minister-president. It is responsible for policy areas that the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) assigns to the states, including education, police, and culture. Federalism thus ensures independent political governance at the state level alongside the federal government.

matches.text: Was hat jedes deutsche Bundesland?
matches.translation: What does every German federal state have?

terms.context: The State Centre for Civic Education is a state institution at the state level with the legal mandate to provide civic education to all citizens regardless of party affiliation or ideology. terms.context: The Landtag is the elected parliament of a German Bundesland (state). It passes state laws, adopts the state budget, and elects the state's head of government. Each of the 16 Bundesländer has its own Landtag and thus an independent parliamentary representation.

terms.context: Life insurance is a private, voluntarily obtained form of financial protection that provides coverage in case of death or upon reaching a certain age. terms.context: The legislature is the legislative branch of government and is responsible for enacting laws that are binding on all. In the German Grundgesetz (Basic Law), it is established as an independent branch and separated from the executive and judiciary—a principle intended to prevent abuse of power. At the federal level, the Bundestag and Bundesrat primarily exercise this function.

terms.context: An apprenticeship position is a training place in Germany's dual vocational education system, which combines on-the-job training with classroom instruction. terms.context: Wage tax is the income tax for employees; the employer calculates it and submits it directly to the tax office. terms.context: The Luftbrücke (Berlin Airlift) refers to the Western Allied supply flights to West Berlin, which were established from 1948 to 1949 in response to the Soviet blockade of all land and water routes. terms.context: Makkabi is an international Jewish sports movement that emerged in the late 19th century in the context of modern Zionism and combines sport with Jewish culture and community. terms.context: A majority means that more than half of the votes in a parliament or chamber fall on one side. terms.context: An opinion is a value judgment or personal view protected by the freedom of opinion enshrined in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). terms.context: Human dignity is established as inviolable in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and thus stands at the head of all fundamental rights. terms.context: A characteristic is a distinguishing individual element that sets a concept, institution, or system apart from others. matches.translation: What is not a feature of our democracy?

terms.context: A migrant is a person who has relocated their permanent residence from another country to Germany. Migration has historically shaped German society—for instance, through state-organized recruitment of foreign workers from the 1960s onward, which led to the formation of permanent immigrant communities. Germany today understands itself as an immigration country in which migrants actively shape social, cultural, and economic life.

matches.translation: From which country did most migrants come to Germany?

terms.context: Ministers each lead a federal ministry and are members of the Bundesregierung (federal government) together with the Bundeskanzler (federal chancellor). terms.context: The Ministerium für Staatssicherheit (Ministry for State Security, Stasi) was the domestic intelligence agency of the DDR (East Germany) and one of the most extensive surveillance systems in history—it systematically gathered information about its own population and persecuted political dissidents. terms.context: The Ministerpräsident (Minister-President) leads the state government of a Bundesland (federal state) and is the highest executive office at the state level. In the city-states of Berlin, Hamburg, and Bremen, this office carries different titles: Regierender Bürgermeister (Governing Mayor), Erste Bürgermeisterin (First Mayor), or Bürgermeister (Mayor). Ministers-President represent their states in the Bundesrat (federal council) and thus participate in federal legislation.

matches.translation: Minister-President

terms.context: In a monarchy, state power rests with a monarch—a king, queen, or emperor—whose office is typically hereditary. Germany was an empire and thus a monarchy until 1918. Since then, the Grundgesetz (Basic Law, German constitution) establishes Germany as a republic in which the head of state is democratically determined.

terms.context: The Monday demonstrations (Montagsdemonstrationen) were regular, nonviolent street protests in the DDR (East Germany) that took place primarily in Leipzig starting in fall 1989. Participants demanded fundamental rights, freedom of movement, and democratic participation. They are considered a central part of the peaceful revolution that led to the fall of the Berlin Wall and German reunification.

matches.translation: at the Monday demonstrations in 1989 in the GDR

terms.context: Maternity protection is a legally anchored safeguard for pregnant women and mothers shortly after childbirth, regulated by the Mutterschutzgesetz (Maternity Protection Act). It includes a prohibition on work in the weeks before and after delivery and a ban on dismissal that prevents pregnancy from being used as grounds for termination. The goal is to ensure the health and economic security of mother and child.

matches.translation: A woman in Germany loses her job. What should not be the reason for this dismissal?

matches.translation: Maternity leave

terms.context: Night quiet refers to the legally protected rest period that typically runs from 10 p.m. to 6 a.m. in Germany. During this time, noise disturbances—such as loud music, home repairs, or celebrations—must be avoided to protect neighbors' right to undisturbed sleep. The specific regulations are set out in state pollution control laws, municipal ordinances, and house rules.

matches.text: Wann beginnt die gesetzliche Nachtruhe in Deutschland?
matches.translation: When does the statutory night rest period begin in Germany?

terms.context: The national anthem is a state's official musical symbol and expression of its shared values. In Germany, the third stanza of the Deutschlandlied (Song of Germany) serves as the national anthem because its key terms—unity, justice, and freedom—correspond to the fundamental principles of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). This stanza was initially adopted for the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic) and confirmed for all of Germany after reunification in 1990.

matches.text: Mit welchen Worten beginnt die deutsche Nationalhymne?
matches.translation: What words does the German national anthem begin with?

terms.context: National Socialism was a totalitarian ideology and form of rule in Germany from 1933 to 1945 based on racism, antisemitism, and the Führerprinzip (leadership principle). The regime completely eliminated democratic institutions, fundamental rights, and the rule of law, and systematically persecuted and murdered millions of people, including Jews, Sinti and Roma, and political opponents. The Holocaust and World War II are the gravest consequences of this dictatorship; remembrance of its victims is a fixed part of German democratic self-understanding.

terms.context: National Socialists were followers of the NSDAP (Nazi Party) and its ideology based on racism, antisemitism, and the Führerprinzip (leadership principle). Under their rule, democratic fundamental rights were abolished and systematic persecution and extermination of people were carried out. The Nazi dictatorship ended in 1945 and serves as the benchmark for why the protection of fundamental rights and democracy in Germany today has constitutional status.

matches.translation: Jewish shops and synagogues are destroyed by Nazis and their supporters.

terms.context: National Socialism (1933–1945) was a dictatorship founded on racism, antisemitism, and terror that led to the systematic persecution and murder of millions of people—including approximately six million Jews in the Holocaust. These crimes against humanity form a central part of German memory culture and political responsibility. From this historical guilt, the Federal Republic of Germany derives lasting consequences for its domestic and foreign policy, such as the obligation to uphold human rights and special responsibility for Israel's security.

matches.text: Woraus begründet sich Deutschlands besondere Verantwortung für Israel?
matches.translation: What is the basis for Germany's special responsibility for Israel?

terms.context: The NS state refers to Nazi Germany under Adolf Hitler's dictatorship from 1933 to 1945. During this period, democratic institutions were destroyed, fundamental rights were abolished, and the state was built on racist ideology, the leadership principle, and totalitarian control. The experiences of this dictatorship fundamentally shaped the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) of the Federal Republic of Germany: it was intended to ensure that such rule would never be possible again.

matches.text: Was kennzeichnete den NS-Staat? Eine Politik …
matches.translation: What characterized the Nazi state? A policy...

terms.context: Public dissemination refers to the communication of a statement or information to an unspecified, potentially unlimited group of people—such as through the press, radio, social media, or public speeches. In law, it is classified as particularly serious because it can directly affect reputation protection, personal rights, and public discourse. Therefore, legal norms, such as those protecting against defamation or establishing media responsibility, specifically attach consequences to public dissemination.

matches.text: Wann ist die Meinungsfreiheit in Deutschland eingeschränkt?
matches.translation: When is freedom of expression restricted in Germany?

terms.context: The opposition consists of the members of parliament who do not belong to the government coalition. Their task is to publicly monitor government action, criticize it, and present alternatives. A functioning opposition is considered an indispensable feature of parliamentary democracy.

matches.translation: In Parliament the term “opposition” stands for…

terms.context: The municipal order office is an agency of local government responsible for enforcing laws and regulations at the local level. It acts on behalf of the municipality and oversees, among other things, noise protection, parking regulations, and business supervision. In this way, it contributes to maintaining public order in the immediate living environment of the population.

matches.translation: Public order office

terms.context: The Eastern Treaties (Ostverträge) are a series of bilateral agreements that the Federal Republic of Germany concluded in the early 1970s with the Soviet Union, Poland, the German Democratic Republic (DDR), and other East European states. They recognized the borders that emerged after World War II under international law and formed the foundation for political détente between Western and Eastern Europe. This policy of rapprochement, known as the New Eastern Policy (Neue Ostpolitik), fundamentally shaped German foreign policy during the Cold War era.

matches.text: Welcher Politiker steht für die "Ostverträge"?
matches.translation: Which politician stands for the “Eastern Treaties”?

terms.context: A parliament is the elected popular representation of a state; in Germany, the Bundestag (federal parliament) assumes this role at the federal level. It passes laws, elects the Federal Chancellor (Bundeskanzlerin or Bundeskanzler), and monitors the work of the Federal Government (Bundesregierung). Only parties that exceed the five-percent threshold or win at least three direct mandates in the election are represented in the Bundestag.

matches.translation: In Parliament the term “opposition” stands for…

terms.context: Parliamentary opposition refers to the factions in parliament that do not belong to the government. Their main task is to critically examine government action, develop alternatives, and publicly expose failures. A functioning opposition is considered an indispensable element of democratic separation of powers and oversight.

matches.translation: The parliamentary opposition in the German Bundestag...

terms.context: A member of parliament belongs to an elected representative body and participates in lawmaking and political decision-making. In Germany, parliaments exist at the federal, state, and local levels, with members receiving their mandate through free elections. They represent the interests of the population, monitor the government, and enact binding laws.

terms.context: Political parties unite societal interests and participate in political decision-making, particularly in the Bundestag, where they introduce and enact laws. Their activity is limited by the Basic Law (Grundgesetz): initiatives that violate the constitution or fundamental rights are legally impermissible. As an expression of societal pluralism, parties compete politically and form a central element of parliamentary democracy.

terms.context: The Basic Law (Grundgesetz) protects the multi-party system as an essential feature of the free democratic basic order—a lesson from the National Socialist dictatorship, which after 1933 banned all parties except the Nazi Party (NSDAP) and thereby destroyed democratic competition. Today, only the Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht) can impose a party ban, if a party aims to abolish the democratic basic order. This regulation is anchored in Article 21, Paragraph 2 of the Basic Law.

matches.translation: When can a party be banned in Germany?
